title: Michelle Tsosie Sisneros: A Brush with Cultural Legacy and Artistic Triumph
subtitle: From Groundbreaking Artistry to the Impact of a Santa Clara/Navajo/Laguna Pueblo Upbringing on the Art World
author: alannalgreen
raw_article:
In the world of art, there exist a few voices that resonate beyond the canvases, painting not just pictures but also shaping cultural narratives. Michelle Tsosie Sisneros is one such artist whose work not only reflects her Pueblo roots but also radiates a sense of transcendence, breaking through the barriers of home and heritage to attain international acclaim. Her journey, from a creative being to a professional artist, is not just an inspiration—it's an ode to resilience, self-discovery, and the healing power of art.

The Prelude of an Artist’s Vision

Born and raised in the vibrant tapestry of Pueblo life, Sisneros's story began much like the generations before her, harmonizing with the earth and traditions. She grew up in Window Rock, Arizona, the capital of the Navajo nation. After spending most of her life in Arizona, Sisneros now resides in Santa Clara Pueblo, New Mexico. She is a highly regarded contemporary artist of Santa Clara, Navajo and Laguna descent. When asked about her upbringing, Michelle wrote in an email,

I have been creative for as long as I can remember, painting and drawing are what I liked to do most. I do not have a schooled background in art… I have many inspirations, and this has changed over the many years I have been creating art; my ability has always been what I like to believe is a gift… People inspire me, but more so my connection to the Earth and especially the places I come from are what ground me as an artist.

Her foray into the world of art was not one of academic pursuit, but an innate calling, a 'gift' as she modestly puts it, a talent so profound it could only have come from a power beyond human endeavor, a blessed companion on her path through life.

Reconciling Two Worlds

From her earliest memories, the canvas and the easel have been her confidants, and through painting and drawing, she found solace and expression in their purest forms. Her artistic romance, however, was not to be kindled until her thirties when the decision to embrace her passion as a profession set her on a path of uncharted challenges. Sisneros realized her calling as an artist; she saw herself as a painter creating a unique blend of culture and sharing it with others. By the time she was in her mid-thirties, the palette of her culture had found its way into her heart, and eventually, onto her canvases. When asked about challenges she has faced as an artist, Sisneros replied,

Challenges as an artist for me have been so wide ranged it is hard to pinpoint one thing… I live between worlds as most Indigenous people do. I understand what my life is living on a reservation and what the challenges are in mainstream America.

As an Indigenous woman, Sisneros reconciles the often-disparate worlds of reservation life and mainstream America. Her art serves as a bridge, speaking volumes in the silences that sometimes occur when crossing cultural divides.

A Palette of Self-Discovery - Messages from the Earth 

Sisneros’s journey isn't just about overcoming, it's about discovery. Her artwork, heavily influenced by the 1940’s Santa Fe Indian School period, isn't just a reflection of her culture - it's an evolution. As Sisneros explains, “I have been heavily influenced by artists who began developing a unique style reminiscent of the 1940’s Santa Fe Indian School period. A style and time which has long disappeared”.

Santa Fe Indian School: Nurturing Identity and Honoring Tradition

Amidst the majestic deserts and adobe architecture of New Mexico lies a crucible of culture and education: the Santa Fe Indian School. Founded in 1890, the school's early years were marred with the painful legacy of assimilation and separation from traditional family structures. The 1940s ushered in a new chapter for the school, where a fledgling Native American Renaissance began to take form. Students wrestled with the mandate to forsake their native languages and customs, yet within this crucible, a seed of resistance and rebirth was sown. The institution shifted from a strict policy of erasing indigenous culture to one of reluctant recognition and eventually, to active celebration.

Embracing the Past to Illuminate the Future: A Cultural Revival Takes Root

The Santa Fe Indian School is not merely an educational institution but a living testament to the indomitable spirit of the Native American people. It has weathered the storms of assimilation, and from the rich soil of adversity, has sprung forth a bountiful legacy of artistic and intellectual legacy. As Sisneros explains,

The Santa Fe Indian School was of course another institution where native children were forced into a western academic assimilation way of life. The one good thing that came from this was the focus on art. A woman by the name of Dorothy Dunn taught students a style of painting known as studio style flat painting and drawing…Painting during this time was frowned upon, especially by women, mainly due to sensitive cultural subjects by the tribes of these students. [Dunn] taught basic fundamentals to her students and what resulted was a style that primarily depicted the students' lives from their respective tribes. As a result, many of these students continued after she left and some gained great notoriety, such as Allen Houser, Pablita Velarde, Pop Chalee, Tonita Pena and others. My great aunt was a student by the name of Pablita Velarde, she went on to gain international acclaim. If not for women like her, artists like me would have had a much greater road to travel.

This period was vital, not just for the students, but for the birth of a movement that would redefine the role of Native American heritage in the nation's cultural fabric. The flame of tribal heritage was kept alive by the teachings of Dorothy Dunn, who instructed students in their respective tribal heritage. Since Dunn, the immersive program has nurtured and launched some of the most significant Native American artists of the 20th century, whose work transcends the canvas, challenging and cherishing the world's understanding of indigenous art.

As a descendant of internationally acclaimed pueblo artist and painter Pablita Verlarde, Sisneros discovered her source of self-confidence in the strokes of her brush. It’s as if the canvas mirrors her growth from a budding artist to one who confidently strokes her signature blend of Pueblo life onto the art she creates.  In her canvases, we find echoes of the collective spirit of her people, the harmonious pull of the land, and the timeless dance between the natural and the supernatural. Her art is not just an expression but a lifeline, tethering her to her culture in pursuit of her goal.

Weaving a Community with Threads of Influence

Sisneros has woven a community of support that ranges from her family, who shares her artistic legacy, to the very earth that inspires her. Her work is a series of dialogues with the Earth and the spirits that dwell within it. Each piece is a conversation, sometimes a lament, sometimes a celebration, but always an acknowledgment of the connectedness that we share with the world we inhabit. Through recurring themes of earth connection and Pueblo culture, Sisneros implores us to pause, to reflect, and to recognize the sanctity of our place within the universe.

Nurturing Roots in the Business of Art, the Fabric of Culture

From humble beginnings, Sisneros has diversified her portfolio, turning art into a tapestry that includes note cards, painted shoes, and illustrations for children’s books. Her work isn't just carried by the wind, but by prestigious outlets and museums, etching her legacy into the very halls of art history.

The artist also delved into the design of textiles and fashion, integrating her paintings into everyday life, keeping her work not just evolutionary, but relevant in an ever-changing world.  

Through her fashion and textile designs, she weaves her vivid paintings into the very fabric of her community. Her sense of design resonates, her flair creating textural experiences that echo through the attires and homes of those who engage with her art.

The Threads of Legacy

Sisneros’s calling as an artist began in the whispers of the wind across the New Mexican mesas, and it has evolved into a resonant voice that transcends her community, speaking to the world at large. She is an artist who understands both the brush and the broom, indulging not in solitude but in the wisdom of the world she paints. Her journey is an aspirational narrative, teaching that passion and dedication can yield bountiful harvests. Her rise to prominence leads by example, showing that challenges and success are threads on the same loom, weaving a tapestry of experiences that are as varied as they are valuable.

The legacy of Michelle Tsosie Sisneros is not one of accolades or wealth; it is a legacy of resilience, innovation, and a profound connection to the very essence of her being. Her art has touched the lives of many, and her entrepreneurial spirit has opened doors for a new generation of artists. The brushstrokes of Michelle Tsosie Sisneros continue to paint a picture of hope, empowerment, and boundless creativity.

Conclusion

Through her work, Sisneros does not merely portray, she preserves. She takes the ephemeral nature of life on the reservation and mainstreams the unheard stories, painting them on walls and pages that will outlive her and resonate with generations to come. Sisneros's art stands as a monument—a testament to the strength of a culture, preserved in the artistic triumph of one resilient spirit. Her life may have been met with challenges, but her art has met the world with courage and grace. The colors she chooses, the stories she tells, and the legacy she weaves are not just bold brushstrokes on a canvas. They are a work of heart, a life set to the rhythm of a timeless culture, and a soulful melody of creation against all odds.

To discover more art and fashion created by Sisneros, please go to her website.

title: Kiliii Yüyan: Unveiling The Extraordinary Through Arctic Stories
subtitle: A Deep Dive into Kiliii Yüyan's Arctic Journeys with National Geographic Live! January 22nd, 2024, at Benaroya Hall in Seattle, Washington.
author: alannalgreen
raw_article:
In the vast, frozen expanses of the Arctic, there exists a world both harsh and beautiful, where stories are as deep as the largest cetaceans inhabiting its uncharted waters. It's here that National Geographic Society explorer, filmmaker, and photographer Kiliii Yüyan found his calling – or rather, where it was handed down to him from the narrative-rich heritage of his Nanai and Chinese-American ancestry.

Kiliii Yüyan: Tracing Indigenous Roots

Kiliii Yüyan's story is a tapestry woven with threads from the Nanai and other Indigenous communities. With proud Nanai lineage, Yüyan's work is an homage to the rivers and forests that have sustained his ancestors for generations. His photography breathes new life into traditional iconography and storytelling, unfurling a vista of cultural exuberance.

Indigenous heritage embodies wisdom, tradition, and an unbreakable bond with the land. For communities like the Nanai people, this bond is the cornerstone of their identity. Their customs, rituals, and artistic endeavors are not just vestiges of the past but living testaments to a resilient spirit that has weathered the tides of modernity.

The Nanai, also known as the Goldi or Hezhe, are an indigenous people native to the Russian Far East and northeastern China. They primarily inhabit the region around the Amur River, which forms the border between Russia and China. The Nanai people have a rich cultural and historical background, and their traditional way of life is closely tied to the rivers and forests of their homeland.

The Nanai traditionally engaged in fishing, hunting, and gathering, relying on the abundant natural resources of the Amur River basin. Their culture includes distinctive artistic expressions, traditional music, dance, and shamanistic practices. Like many indigenous communities, the Nanai have faced challenges related to cultural preservation, language loss, and changes in their environment.

Pioneering into the Frozen Frontier

Yüyan’s journey began not with cameras and honed craft but with the echoes of his Nanai grandmother’s tales, weaving images of brittle ice, the haunting calls of orca whales, and camaraderie in the face of the cold. Yüyan’s career began as a traditional skin-on-kayak builder, a tradition passed down through his Nanai lineage, and which he continues to practice to this day. Building traditional kayaks is, for Yüyan, a way to both honor and celebrate a part of the cultural memory of his Northern Indigenous culture.

This impalpable inheritance was the kindling to a storied career that would take him to the ends of the earth, paddling on a traditional skin-on-frame kayak across treacherous waters and cracking ice with practiced precision.

In the Arctic, Yüyan captures the symbiosis of humans with their environment, where life is as fragile and treacherous as the ice underfoot. Humans have to be very wary of where they place their feet; the ice being so thin that the possibility of breaking through is a very real threat to personal safety. It is in these areas where individuals are always wary and watchful of the quality of the ice that they are on.

These are some of the conditions Yüyan brings to life while discussing his wide body of work published by National Geographic. It is in these moments when his lens turns to the Inupiaq whalers whose stories of survival he brings to life onstage. Throughout his presentation, his deep reverence of the bowhead whale as the great provider remains paramount, embodying a relationship with nature that is at once practical and deeply spiritual.

Yüyan's photographs capture the physical and emotional strength of the Inupiaq people as they hunt for this majestic creature. The images are powerful, conveying a sense of awe at the sheer size and power of the bowhead whale, while also highlighting the skill and bravery of those who pursue it. Beyond the thrill of the hunt, Yüyan's work also sheds light on important cultural practices that have been passed down through generations. He captures moments of traditional ceremonies and celebrations, showcasing the close bond between community members and their deep connection to nature.

Yüyan is not merely a visual storyteller but a profound interpreter of the land’s conservation ethos. In the clash between tradition and modernity, he stands as a bridge between cultures, advocating for the preservation of Indigenous knowledge networks that have sustained life in the Arctic for centuries. His advocacy extends to the geopolitical realm, as he rallies against offshore drilling through the wise voices of the elders – the ecological navigators of his Inupiaq collaborators.

The power of Kiliii Yüyan’s art lies not just in its visual allure but in the salience with which it speaks to the viewer's soul. His images, coupling the dynamic beauty of the Arctic’s ever-changing landscapes with the resilience of its communities, are a call to action. They urge the beholder to respect, honor, and safeguard the ecological and cultural treasures of this polar wilderness.

A Melody for the Whales

One cannot speak of Yüyan without mentioning his mirthful philosophy that embodies his approach to life and work. Drawing from the teachings of his grandmother, he embraces the spiritual connection between all living beings—humans, animals, and the elements—which he personifies by serenading the ocean’s leviathans. During the presentation, Yüyan recalled a fond memory of beckoning an orca calf to the brim of his kayak while gently singing the words to The Beatles’ “Hey Jude” aloud. This seemingly whimsical act is, in fact, a poignant symbol of the intimate connection he seeks between his subjects and himself, one rooted in understanding, empathy, and shared existence.

As we navigate a world beset by environmental crises, Kiliii Yüyan reminds us that every part of the natural world has a voice – and by listening, we can learn how to protect our planet and our collective heritage.

Notable Highlights from Nat Geo Live! include:

- Kiliii Yüyan believes in the powerful connection between humans and the natural world, a principle inspired by his Nanai and Chinese-American ancestry.

- He upholds the philosophy of conservation, especially evident in his work with the sealing community in Northern Greenland.

- He emphasizes the importance of education and self-awareness when advocating for Indigenous communities.

- Yüyan insists on building trust and relationships with communities he photographs, advocating humility and patience as key aspects in this process.

- He acknowledges the profound effects of climate change on mental health within the Arctic community, advocating for the use of Indigenous elders' wisdom over external therapists.

body.content: In the vast, frozen expanses of the Arctic, there exists a world both harsh and beautiful, where stories are as deep as the largest cetaceans inhabiting its uncharted waters.  It's here that National Geographic Society explorer, filmmaker, and photographer Kiliii Yüyan found his calling – or rather, where it was handed down to him from the narrative-rich heritage of his Nanai and Chinese-American ancestry.
Kiliii Yüyan: Tracing Indigenous Roots

Kiliii Yüyan's story is a tapestry woven with threads from the Nanai and other Indigenous communities.  With proud Nanai lineage, Yüyan's work is an homage to the rivers and forests that have sustained his ancestors for generations.  His photography breathes new life into traditional iconography and storytelling, unfurling a vista of cultural exuberance.

Indigenous heritage embodies wisdom, tradition, and an unbreakable bond with the land.  For communities like the Nanai people, this bond is the cornerstone of their identity.  Their customs, rituals, and artistic endeavors are not just vestiges of the past but living testaments to a resilient spirit that has weathered the tides of modernity.

The Nanai, also known as the Goldi or Hezhe, are an indigenous people native to the Russian Far East and northeastern China.  They primarily inhabit the region around the Amur River, which forms the border between Russia and China.  The Nanai people have a rich cultural and historical background, and their traditional way of life is closely tied to the rivers and forests of their homeland.

The Nanai traditionally engaged in fishing, hunting, and gathering, relying on the abundant natural resources of the Amur River basin.  Their culture includes distinctive artistic expressions, traditional music, dance, and shamanistic practices. Like many indigenous communities, the Nanai have faced challenges related to cultural preservation, language loss, and changes in their environment.
Pioneering into the Frozen Frontier

Yüyan’s journey began not with cameras and honed craft but with the echoes of his Nanai grandmother’s tales, weaving images of brittle ice, the haunting calls of orca whales, and camaraderie in the face of the cold.  Yüyan’s career began as a traditional skin-on-kayak builder, a tradition passed down through his Nanai lineage, and which he continues to practice to this day.  Building traditional kayaks is, for Yüyan, a way to both honor and celebrate a part of the cultural memory of his Northern Indigenous culture.

This impalpable inheritance was the kindling to a storied career that would take him to the ends of the earth, paddling on a traditional skin-on-frame kayak across treacherous waters and cracking ice with practiced precision.

In the Arctic, Yüyan captures the symbiosis of humans with their environment, where life is as fragile and treacherous as the ice underfoot.  Humans have to be very wary of where they place their feet; the ice being so thin that the possibility of breaking through is a very real threat to personal safety.  It is in these areas where individuals are always wary and watchful of the quality of the ice that they are on.

These are some of the conditions Yüyan brings to life while discussing his wide body of work published byNational Geographic.  It is in these moments when his lens turns to the Inupiaq whalers whose stories of survival he brings to life onstage.  Throughout his presentation, his deep reverence of the bowhead whale as the great provider remains paramount, embodying a relationship with nature that is at once practical and deeply spiritual.

Yüyan's photographs capture the physical and emotional strength of the Inupiaq people as they hunt for this majestic creature.  The images are powerful, conveying a sense of awe at the sheer size and power of the bowhead whale, while also highlighting the skill and bravery of those who pursue it.  Beyond the thrill of the hunt, Yüyan's work also sheds light on important cultural practices that have been passed down through generations.  He captures moments of traditional ceremonies and celebrations, showcasing the close bond between community members and their deep connection to nature.

Yüyan is not merely a visual storyteller but a profound interpreter of the land’s conservation ethos.  In the clash between tradition and modernity, he stands as a bridge between cultures, advocating for the preservation of Indigenous knowledge networks that have sustained life in the Arctic for centuries.  His advocacy extends to the geopolitical realm, as he rallies against offshore drilling through the wise voices of the elders – the ecological navigators of his Inupiaq collaborators.

The power of Kiliii Yüyan’s art lies not just in its visual allure but in the salience with which it speaks to the viewer's soul.  His images, coupling the dynamic beauty of the Arctic’s ever-changing landscapes with the resilience of its communities, are a call to action.  They urge the beholder to respect, honor, and safeguard the ecological and cultural treasures of this polar wilderness.
A Melody for the Whales

One cannot speak of Yüyan without mentioning his mirthful philosophy that embodies his approach to life and work.  Drawing from the teachings of his grandmother, he embraces the spiritual connection between all living beings—humans, animals, and the elements—which he personifies by serenading the ocean’s leviathans.  During the presentation, Yüyan recalled a fond memory of beckoning an orca calf to the brim of his kayak while gently singing the words to The Beatles’ “Hey Jude” aloud.  This seemingly whimsical act is, in fact, a poignant symbol of the intimate connection he seeks between his subjects and himself, one rooted in understanding, empathy, and shared existence.

As we navigate a world beset by environmental crises, Kiliii Yüyan reminds us that every part of the natural world has a voice – and by listening, we can learn how to protect our planet and our collective heritage.
